summary:
The [[event:2300|Hanseatic League]] reached its peak power and geographic extent during the 14th and early 15th centuries, when the confederation of northern European merchant cities controlled Baltic and North Sea trade so thoroughly that it could impose embargoes on entire kingdoms and fight successful wars against Scandinavian monarchs. The League's defining moment came with the Treaty of Stralsund (1370), which concluded the First Hanseatic-Danish War: the Hanseatic cities, led by Lübeck, defeated King Valdemar IV of Denmark who had sacked the major Hanseatic trading station at Visby on the island of Gotland. The peace terms gave the Hansa cities unprecedented privileges in Denmark and Norway — including the right to approve or veto the Danish royal succession — representing the commercial confederation's maximum political power over sovereign states.

At its 14th-century peak, the Hanseatic League comprised some 70–170 cities (estimates vary depending on the definition of membership) stretching from the Rhine to the eastern Baltic, with trading outposts (Kontore) in London (the Steelyard), Bruges, Bergen, and Novgorod. Lübeck served as the League's effective capital, hosting the Hansetage (general assembly) where member cities negotiated policy. The League's commercial infrastructure — standardized contracts, shared legal codes, letters of credit, and a fleet of the distinctive Hanse cog (a broad-beamed cargo vessel) — created the most sophisticated commercial network in northern Europe. The League's dominance began declining in the late 15th century as the rising nation-states of England, Denmark, and the Netherlands developed their own merchant fleets and commercial policies capable of challenging Hanseatic monopolies.
